document_text:
## How stdio JSON-RPC transports work in MCP

The Model Context Protocol uses several transport mechanisms to move messages between a client (an AI host application) and a server (a tool provider). The simplest and most widely deployed is the standard input/output transport, commonly called stdio. Under stdio, the client launches the server as a subprocess and communicates with it through the server's stdin and stdout file descriptors. Each message is a single line of JSON, terminated by a newline character. The format follows the JSON-RPC 2.0 specification: every request includes a method name, optional parameters, and an id used to correlate responses; every response includes the matching id and either a result or an error object.

The stdio transport has practical advantages that explain its prevalence. There is no network configuration, no port allocation, and no authentication layer to design — the client launching the subprocess implicitly grants it the same trust boundary the client itself runs in. The transport also has no protocol-level discovery: the client knows where the binary lives because the user told it, typically through a configuration file. In Claude Desktop the configuration lives in claude_desktop_config.json under the mcpServers key; each entry specifies the command to launch and any environment variables the server needs.

Message handling on the server side follows a consistent pattern. The server reads one line at a time from stdin, parses it as JSON, dispatches it to the appropriate handler based on the method field, and writes the response back to stdout as a single line of JSON. Logging output goes to stderr so it does not interfere with the message stream. A well-implemented server flushes stdout after every response to avoid buffering delays that would manifest as apparent latency to the client.

The protocol defines several primitive operations that every server must implement. The initialize handshake is the first message a client sends; the server responds with its protocol version, server name, and version string. After initialization the client can call tools/list to discover what tools the server provides, resources/list for resources, and prompts/list for prompt templates. Each tool can be invoked through tools/call with a name and an arguments object. Resources are fetched through resources/read with a URI. Prompts are retrieved through prompts/get with a name and optional arguments.

Servers also receive notifications, which are JSON-RPC messages without an id. The most common notification is initialized, which the client sends after the handshake to signal it is ready to start sending requests. Servers do not send responses to notifications; the absence of an id is the cue that the message expects no reply.

Error handling at the transport level is minimal because most failure modes are protocol-level rather than transport-level. If the server crashes, the client detects this through stdin closing and can choose to relaunch. If the server returns an error response, the error object includes a code and a message that the client surfaces to the user.

provenance.rationale: H3 reproducibility test requires only that the document produce non-trivial LLM analysis output. Doc choice is methodologically neutral for H3 (the measurement is reproducibility-of-the-analyzer, not quality-of-the-analysis). This is a synthetic technical-descriptive document about how MCP stdio transports work — analytical prose, English, no recommendation, no opinion content, no positioning leak risk.
